distractor_rationales: This returns the argument \( \cos\theta \) itself instead of \( \sin\theta \).
This uses \( \sqrt{1+\cos^2\theta} \) with the wrong sign inside the root.
This stops at \( 1-\cos^2\theta \) without taking the square root.
This computes \( \tan\theta \) rather than \( \sin\theta \).
This has the wrong sign; \( \sin\theta \ge 0 \) on \( [0,\pi] \).
This is off by one from the correct value.

This uses \( \sqrt{1+\sin^2\theta} \) with the wrong sign inside the root.
This stops at \( 1-\sin^2\theta \) without taking the square root.
This computes \( \tan\theta \) rather than \( \cos\theta \).
This has the wrong sign; \( \cos\theta \ge 0 \) on \( [-\tfrac{\pi}{2},\tfrac{\pi}{2}] \).

This returns \( \cos\theta \) instead of \( \tan\theta \).
This uses \( \sqrt{1+\sin^2\theta} \) with the wrong sign inside the root.

solution: Let \( \theta = \arccos\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \), so \( \cos\theta = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \) and \( \sin\theta = \sqrt{1-\cos^2\theta} \) (nonnegative on \( [0,\pi] \)). Then \( \tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = [[answer]] \); the sign follows the sign of \( [[a]] \) since \( \theta \) lands in Quadrant I or II.
distractor_rationales: This returns \( \cos\theta \) instead of \( \tan\theta \).
This returns \( \sin\theta \) instead of \( \tan\theta \).
This uses \( \sqrt{1+\cos^2\theta} \) with the wrong sign inside the root.
This stops at \( 1-\cos^2\theta \) without finishing the ratio.
This has the wrong sign for \( \tan\theta \).
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Let \( \theta = \arctan\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \). Build a right triangle with opposite leg \( [[a]] \) and adjacent leg \( [[b]] \), so the hypotenuse is \( \sqrt{[[a]]^2+[[b]]^2} \). Then \( \sin\theta = \dfrac{[[a]]}{\sqrt{[[a]]^2+[[b]]^2}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This returns the input ratio \( \tan\theta \) itself instead of \( \sin\theta \).
This uses \( a^2-b^2 \) under the root instead of \( a^2+b^2 \).
This swaps the opposite and adjacent legs, giving \( \cos\theta \) instead of \( \sin\theta \).
This reports the hypotenuse itself rather than a ratio.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: Let \( A=\arcsin\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \) (so \( \sin A=\tfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}, \cos A=\sqrt{1-(\tfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]})^2}\ge 0 \)) and \( B=\arccos\dfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]]} \) (so \( \cos B=\tfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]]}, \sin B=\sqrt{1-(\tfrac{[[c]]}{[[d]]})^2}\ge 0 \)). By the angle-addition identity, \( \sin(A+B)=\sin A\cos B+\cos A\sin B = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This adds the two ratios directly instead of applying the sum identity.
This multiplies the two ratios instead of applying the sum identity.
This uses a difference of sines rather than the angle-addition formula.
This computes \( \cos(A+B) \) (uses the cosine sum identity's sign pattern) instead of \( \sin(A+B) \).
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct value.
